bibliographic_data.title: Diversity-Aware Reverse Kullback-Leibler Divergence for Large Language Model Distillation
bibliographic_data.abstract: Reverse Kullback–Leibler (RKL) divergence has recently emerged as the preferred objective for large language model (LLM) distillation, consistently outperforming forward KL (FKL), particularly in regimes with large vocabularies and significant teacher–student capacity mismatch, where RKL focuses learning on dominant modes rather than enforcing dense alignment. However, RKL introduces a structural limitation that drives the student toward overconfident predictions. To address these issues, we propose Diversity-aware RKL (DRKL), which removes this gradient effect and strengthens non-target supervision while preserving the optimization benefits of RKL. Extensive experiments across datasets and model families demonstrate that DRKL consistently outperforms FKL, RKL, and other state-of-the-art distillation objectives, achieving better performance and a superior fidelity–diversity trade-off.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes Diversity-aware RKL (DRKL) to address the limitations of Reverse Kullback-Leibler (RKL) divergence in large language model distillation, specifically focusing on overconfidence and reduced output diversity.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors decompose the gradients of RKL into target and non-target components to analyze its behavior. They propose DRKL to remove the contribution of non-target gradients to the target logit while strengthening non-target supervision.
outcomes: DRKL consistently outperforms FKL, RKL, and other state-of-the-art distillation objectives across multiple datasets and model families.
DRKL achieves better performance and a superior fidelity–diversity trade-off.
core_content.full_text_markdown:
# Diversity-Aware Reverse Kullback-Leibler Divergence for Large Language Model Distillation

## Abstract
Reverse Kullback–Leibler (RKL) divergence has recently emerged as the preferred objective for large language model (LLM) distillation, consistently outperforming forward KL (FKL), particularly in regimes with large vocabularies and significant teacher–student capacity mismatch. However, RKL introduces a structural limitation that drives the student toward overconfident predictions. To address these issues, we propose Diversity-aware RKL (DRKL), which removes this gradient effect and strengthens non-target supervision while preserving the optimization benefits of RKL. Extensive experiments across datasets and model families demonstrate that DRKL consistently outperforms FKL, RKL, and other state-of-the-art distillation objectives, achieving better performance and a superior fidelity–diversity trade-off.

## Introduction
Knowledge distillation (KD) aims to transfer knowledge from large language models (LLMs) to smaller, more efficient students. While forward KL (FKL) has been the standard objective in early distillation work, recent LLM distillation methods increasingly favor reverse KL (RKL) due to its superior empirical performance. However, prior work suggests that FKL and RKL converge to the same optimum under sufficient optimization, implying that their differences mainly manifest during early training.

In this work, we argue that the advantage of RKL arises from its alignment with the structure of LLMs, rather than being limited to the early stages of training. The theoretical equivalence between FKL and RKL relies on assumptions (e.g., sufficient student capacity, exact optimization to the global optimum) that rarely hold in modern LLM distillation, particularly under large vocabularies and significant teacher–student capacity mismatch. In such settings, FKL enforces probability matching across the entire vocabulary, requiring the student to approximate the teacher's long-tail distribution, which is inherently challenging for capacity-limited models. In contrast, RKL focuses learning on high-probability tokens while suppressing low-probability mass, effectively reducing the complexity of the alignment problem.

Despite these advantages, RKL introduces fundamental limitations. First, it creates a shortcut that rapidly minimizes the non-target loss by increasing the confidence of the target class, without properly aligning the non-target distributions of the teacher and student. As a result, knowledge transfer is incomplete, since the RKL objective can remain low despite poor non-target alignment. Second, by concentrating probability mass on dominant tokens, it systematically reduces the entropy of the student distribution, resulting in overconfident predictions and diminished output diversity.

## Proposed Methodology
To better understand these behaviors, we theoretically and empirically analyze RKL by decomposing the gradients into target and non-target components. Our analysis reveals a distinct mechanism: non-target gradients consistently push the target logit upward, continuing to increase it even after the student's target probability matches that of the teacher, which explains both limitations. Motivated by this observation, we propose Diversity-aware RKL (DRKL), which removes the contribution of non-target gradients to the target logit and strengthens non-target supervision while preserving the optimization benefits of RKL.

## Experiments
Extensive experiments across multiple datasets and model families show that DRKL consistently improves the fidelity–diversity trade-off.
